Tafseer:
Verses (1)
'Ta Seen' is the Qur'anic name of this Surah.

The Qur'an is its own proof and argument:
'These are the verses of the Qur'an and a clear Book' (Tilka aayatul-Qur'ani wa Kitabim-mubeen). The word 'Qur'an' is well-known for the divine Book, and the accompanying attribute 'mubeen' (clear/manifest) shows that it is such a self-evident argument for its every claim that there is no need for any external evidence, miracle, or sign to evaluate its truth and authenticity. This attribute is mentioned here keeping in view those objectors and opponents who were demanding a sign to verify it. Here, both these words have clarified its aspect of grandeur as well as its aspect of being an absolute argument. The meaning is that this is the Qur'an revealed by God, not something to be taken lightly or mocked. From the perspective of establishing proof (itmaam al-hujjah), it is complete in its own existence; so those who wish to dismiss it with mockery should consider what the consequences of their actions might be!

The Qur'an as guidance and glad tidings:
That is, whether its opponents value it or not, this Book has been revealed as a source of guidance and glad tidings for those who have believed. It guides them to the straight path of life and also gives them the glad tidings of success and salvation in both this world and the hereafter. Establishing prayer (iqamat as-salat) and giving charity (ita' az-zakat) are mentioned here as the comprehensive qualities of these believers. We have mentioned at another place that these two practices hold the status of being the binding thread of all virtues in religion. Whether they relate to the rights of Allah (Huquq-ullah) or the rights of people (Huquq-ul-ibad), mentioning them is akin to mentioning all virtues.

Only those who believe in the afterlife will believe in this Qur'an:
The style of restriction and emphasis in 'And they, of the Hereafter, are certain' (Wa hum bil-akhirati hum yoofinoon) points to the reality that those who establish prayer and give charity are, in truth, the ones who have certain faith in the hereafter, and it is this very certainty that has enabled them to believe in this Book. As for those upon whom these practices weigh heavily, they are devoid of certainty in the hereafter, and such people will remain deprived of belief in this Book.

The primary cause of the deniers' rejection:
This points to the actual cause of the deniers' rejection: those who do not believe in the hereafter and consider this worldly life to be the entirety of existence. Their deeds have been made so fair-seeming in their eyes that they are now completely unprepared to think or understand beyond them. This points to the divine law (Sunnah of Allah), which has been explained at various places in this book: when people make this world their sole objective and exhaust all their intellect and capabilities in its pursuit, Allah lets their deeds and worldly preoccupations dominate them in such a way that they are neither willing nor able to abandon them. They continue to wander blindly within them until the angel of death suddenly seizes them.

Punishment befell the deniers of the Messenger in this world too:
Since the punishment of the hereafter is mentioned independently in this verse, contextual evidence shows that 'the worst of punishment' (soo'ul-adhab) relates to worldly punishment. Here, the mention is of the deniers of the Messenger, and as we have explained in its respective place, a decisive punishment inevitably strikes the deniers of a Messenger in this world as well. Therefore, it is said that for them there is a painful punishment in this world, and in the hereafter, they will be the greatest losers. The style of restriction and emphasis in 'And they, in the Hereafter, are the greatest losers' (Wa hum fil-akhirati humul-akhsaroon) is identical to the one in 'And they, of the Hereafter, are certain' (Wa hum bil-akhirati hum yoofinoon) explained above. The meaning is that since these people spent their entire lives making this world their sole objective and never paid heed to the hereafter—even mocking the Qur'an out of their love for this world—they will certainly be the greatest losers in the hereafter!

Consolation for the Holy Prophet:
This is consolation for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): if these people do not believe and persist in their opposition, do not worry about them. Rest assured that this message is being revealed to you by the One who is All-Wise (Hakeem) and All-Knowing (Aleem). Every act of His is based on wisdom, and He is fully aware of everything; therefore, whatever His knowledge and wisdom demand will come to pass, and in that lies what is best for you. Since the All-Wise, All-Knowing Lord is revealing this message to you, He will not leave you alone; rather, He will guide you at every step and lead you to your destination.

The upcoming subject... Verses 7-14
Next is a brief reference to that part of Prophet Moses' story when Allah blessed him with prophethood and, arming him with nine miracles, instructed him to go to Pharaoh and his people to warn them. Relating this story is intended to draw the Holy Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) attention to three key points:

First: Allah has blessed you with the revelation of the Qur'an just as He blessed Moses with prophethood. This is a special favor bestowed upon you; neither should you feel overwhelmed by this unique spiritual experience, nor is there any reason for others to be terrified by it.

Second: Those who do not wish to believe remain spiritually blind even after witnessing the greatest miracles of the world. Allah granted Moses not one or two, but nine distinct miracles, and those to whom they were shown were internally convinced of their divine origin, yet they still refused to believe.

Third: Allah inevitably destroys the agents of corruption (mufsideen) after granting them respite for a specified period. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

Lessons for the Holy Prophet from the story of Moses:
This story of Moses has appeared in previous Surahs where each of its details has been explained. Here, we will focus only on its connection with the central theme of this Surah. Prophethood and messengership are not ordinary human experiences. This experience has been granted only to those whom Allah chose for this special task. Consequently, even those who received it took some time to become familiar with its mysteries, and those to whom they presented their call generally regarded it as something strange and raised various doubts and objections. The only remedy for both these challenges was to relate the accounts of past prophets who had already gone through all these stages, so that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) might find reassurance through their experiences, and the proof would be established against his opponents. Among all the past messengers, Prophet Moses was the most closely resembling of them to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The details of his life were preserved in the Torah, and a nation claiming faith in him was present, which unfortunately was opposing the Qur'an and the Prophet (as will be pointed out later in this Surah). Therefore, the experiences of Moses could serve as the greatest source of comfort for the Prophet and the most instructive lesson for his opponents, provided they made an effort to learn from them. This story is related for both these purposes. Though the Prophet is the direct addressee, the discourse is also directed at the opponents.

Prophethood is a divine gift:
This verse makes it clear that prophethood and messengership are special gifts of divine grace. They cannot be acquired through effort, search, or aspiration. Writers, poets, speakers, fortune-tellers, sorcerers, astrologers, and similar professionals struggle to learn their respective crafts and spend their lives pursuing them to attain some proficiency. However, Allah trains a prophet directly under His own supervision. A prophet is neither anyone's disciple nor does he sit in any school, nor does he harbor any aspiration for prophethood. Rather, Allah Himself prepares him for the specific duty entrusted to him by leading him through the trials of life. Prophet Joseph was trained in the prison of Egypt. Prophet David said of himself that the Lord took him from the sheepfolds to seat him on the throne of Israel. Similarly, Prophet Moses, whose story is being related here, was tending the sheep of Prophet Shu'ayb in Madyan. While returning from there to Egypt on a cold, dark night, he lost his way. Seeing a fire from a distance, he told his family: 'Stay here; I have spotted a fire. I will go there, and if I find people, I will ask for directions; otherwise, I will at least bring a brand of fire so that you can warm yourselves in this cold.'

The call of the Lord of the worlds to Moses:
Moses went to fetch a brand of fire, but there he heard a voice saying: 'Blessed is the one in the fire and whoever is around it.' The passive form of the verb indicates vagueness and grandeur—meaning Moses heard a voice but could not immediately determine whose voice it was or where it originated from. 'Blessed is the one in the fire and whoever is around it' (An boorika man fin-nari wa man hawlaha) is the explanation of that voice: this is not an ordinary fire, but under the veil of this light, the blessed Essence of Allah has manifested Himself in the presence of His blessed angels. Referencing His own blessing and that of His angels at this juncture was not merely to state a fact, but also to reassure Moses. This moment was incredibly overwhelming for Moses. Hearing this voice completely contrary to his expectations on a dark night in the valley must have left him bewildered, and his state would have been even more intense had the first words not been a glad tiding of blessing. This blessed statement must have given Moses great reassurance that there was no danger behind the voice, but rather it had come from the source of all goodness and blessing.

'And exalted is Allah, Lord of the worlds' (Wa subhanalla-hi Rabbil-Alameen) is a phrase of glorification and serves to further emphasize the previous statement. It means that the Lord of the worlds is free from all imperfections, and it is His glory that has manifested before you, so place your trust in Him. He will deal with you with absolute grace, and everything is in His power.

Introduction of the attributes 'Al-Aziz' and 'Al-Hakeem':
The previous words were heard from the unseen, and now the Lord of Honor formally introduced Himself to Moses: 'Indeed, I am Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise' (Innahu Anallahul-Azizul-Hakeem). The purpose of introducing these attributes was obviously not because Moses was unaware of them. Having been raised in the house of Pharaoh and having spent a long time in the company of Prophet Shu'ayb, Moses was well aware of Allah's attributes. Rather, reminding him of these attributes was intended to strengthen his heart for the heavy mission he was about to be assigned. In the previous Surah, the attributes 'Al-Aziz' and 'Al-Rahim' appeared repeatedly, and we have explained their implications. Here, 'Al-Aziz' (Mighty) is coupled with 'Al-Hakeem' (Wise), highlighting that the One who has blessed you with His direct discourse is dominant over all things—none can hinder His will—and He is Wise, meaning every plan of His is based on ultimate wisdom. The style of the sentence also implies that there is no room to imagine any power matching His power, or any wisdom matching His wisdom. Such recognition of His attributes was absolutely essential for carrying the heavy responsibilities Moses was about to face, as mentioned in the upcoming verses. It was this recognition of God's attributes that infused Moses with the determination to stand against a tyrant like Pharaoh alongside a weak nation like the Children of Israel, and Allah granted him victory at every step.

A highly significant aspect regarding the prophets:
The previous verses contain teachings to prepare Moses mentally and spiritually for his mission. Following this, he was blessed with those great outward signs essential for facing Pharaoh. The most significant among these was his staff, from which all subsequent signs manifested. This miracle has been explained elsewhere, where we noted that at the very first instance, Moses was so terrified that he fled without looking back. This aspect of the event is highlighted here to show that a prophet's initial spiritual experiences are completely unfamiliar and overwhelming, causing him to feel anxious at first, until Allah gradually reassures him. This is proof that the prophets did not receive this status through personal desire or aspiration; rather, it manifested entirely contrary to their expectations, and they carried its heavy responsibilities with great caution and concern as a divine duty only after attaining complete reassurance that these experiences were indeed from God and that they were formally commissioned by Him. The experiences of our Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) at the beginning of his prophethood and his initial state are alluded to in the final group of Surahs, and we will explain them in their respective places.

Reassurance for Moses:
'O Moses, do not fear. Indeed, in My presence the messengers do not fear' (Ya Musa la takhaf, innee la yakhafu ladayyal-mursaloon). When Moses fled in fear upon seeing his staff turn into a snake, Allah reassured him: the danger from this staff is not for you, but for your enemies. In the presence of Allah, His messengers have nothing to fear; the real danger is for those who refuse to believe in them. The meaning is: you are a messenger of God, and this staff is a weapon for you, so why should you fear it? Your enemies are the ones who should fear it!

Dispelling an apprehension of Moses:
It is characteristic of the righteous and pure-natured servants of Allah that whenever they face an overwhelming situation, their minds immediately turn to their own past shortcomings, fearing it might be a consequence of them. It is possible that Moses' mind turned to the incident of the Coptic man's killing. Although Moses had repented and Allah had accepted his repentance, the prophets and the righteous are extremely cautious in self-reckoning. Therefore, to reinforce the reassuring promise of 'Indeed, in My presence the messengers do not fear,' Allah comforted Moses by reminding him that if any of His servants commits a mistake and then follows it with repentance and reform, He forgives him, for He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful. This is stated in a general form to show its universal application: 'This is not specific to you; I deal with all My servants in this manner.'

The second sign for Moses:
This is the second sign granted to him on that occasion, which has also been explained in Surah al-A'raf and Surah Ta-Ha. The condition 'it will come out white, without disease' (takhruj bayda'a min ghayri soo') was to remove any concern Moses might have that the whiteness of his hand was due to some ailment. The Qur'an's style shows that this reassurance was given to him at the very moment he was granted the miracle, which is consistent with reason. Had this reassurance not been given, Moses would have felt the same anxiety with this miracle as he did with the staff. This implicitly refutes the Torah's claim that the whiteness of Moses' hand resembled leprosy, a point we have discussed in the commentary of Surah Ta-Ha.

The remaining miracles of Moses manifested through his staff:
'Among nine signs to Pharaoh and his people' (Fee tis'i aayatin ila Fir'awna wa qawmihi). The verb 'go' (idhab) is omitted before this phrase, meaning: 'Go, armed with nine signs, to Pharaoh and his people to warn them, for they are indeed a disobedient people.' Initially, Moses was granted only two signs—the staff and the white hand—but these contained many other signs that manifested in the subsequent stages. The details of these signs, along with references from the Torah, have been discussed in the commentary of Surah al-Baqarah, Surah al-A'raf, and Surah Ta-Ha. All these signs manifested through Moses' staff; thus, in the form of the staff, Moses was effectively given a complete arsenal of signs for his struggle against Pharaoh. It should also be noted that these nine signs refer only to those shown to Pharaoh and his people; the signs shown specifically to the Children of Israel through this staff are distinct and numerous, as recorded in the Torah.

The miracles of the prophets are eye-opening:
'Mubsirah' means signs that open the eyes. The miracles manifested through the prophets were so clear that their divine origin was obvious to all. Their overwhelming nature opened the eyes of even the most simple-minded, but those who refused to believe dismissed Moses' miracles as mere magic. Even when the state magicians openly acknowledged that this was not magic, the stubborn deniers persisted in calling it magic. Recall the point explained earlier: the peak of a magician's craft is to make a rope appear to slide like a snake, and he undergoes great effort to learn this trick and feels proud of it. In contrast, Moses was so terrified when he saw his staff turn into a snake that he fled and found no rest until Allah reassured him. Yet, the wrongdoers still called him a master magician and labeled his signs as 'clear magic.'

وجحدوا بھا واستیف تھا انفسھم ظلماً وعلوا فانظر کیف کان فیہ المفسدین (14)
Injustice and arrogance are the actual causes of rejection:
At the end, the primary lesson for which this story is related is presented. Addressing the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it is stated that those who do not wish to accept the truth will seek excuses even after witnessing nine distinct miracles. Their hearts are convinced of the truth of the caller, but their tongues continue to reject him. The actual cause of their rejection is not that the truth is unclear to them, but rather their injustice (zulm) and arrogance (uluww). They are accustomed to violating the rights of God and His servants and do not wish to abandon their privileges. Their arrogance prevents them from bowing before any truth that goes against their desires. The end of such corrupt agents is always the same as that of Pharaoh and his people.

The upcoming subject... Verses 15-44
The previous verses made it clear that the actual cause of rejecting the truth is not the obscurity of reality, but human injustice and arrogance. When Allah blesses people with His favors, these favors often become a trial for them; instead of becoming grateful and obedient servants, they turn arrogant and spread corruption on earth. Now, the righteous servants of Allah are mentioned: the more blessings they receive from Allah, the more their heads bow down in humility before Him, like a branch laden with fruit. As a practical example of this character, Prophets David and Solomon (peace be upon them) are presented. Allah granted them such majesty and authority as was never granted to any of their contemporary rulers, and subjected such resources to them as were never subjected to anyone else. Yet, receiving all this did not lead them to any injustice or arrogance; rather, they treated every blessing as a trial, strove to fulfill its rights, and succeeded in this trial. Read the verses in this light.

Meaning of 'knowledge':
Knowledge refers to the wisdom and recognition granted to David and Solomon (peace be upon them), as reflected in the Psalms (Zabur) and the Proverbs, as well as the scientific knowledge through which they established a mighty empire that was the most powerful land and sea force of its time. The details of this have been discussed in Surah al-Anbiya.

The gratitude of David and Solomon:
'And they said: "Praise be to Allah, who..."' (Wa qala al-hamdu lillahil-ladhee...). This is an expression of their deep gratitude. Despite being the rulers of the most powerful empire of their time and the leaders in technological advancements, they never harbored any pride that these achievements were due to their personal efforts. Rather, they remained grateful to their Lord for blessing them with this honor and status. The words 'over many of His believing servants' carry the reality that true honor and status lie in faith (iman); for those who lack it, there is no question of true honor. It is indeed a matter of high status that Allah elevates someone among His believing servants.

The aerial force of Prophet Solomon:
Meaning, Solomon succeeded David. As illustrious as the father was, the son was even more so. We have explained in Surah al-Anbiya that alongside his land forces, Solomon developed his naval power significantly, making his navy the most powerful in the world. This and the upcoming verses make it clear that he was also blessed with a special knowledge of the language of birds, and by training them, he utilized them for carrying messages, gathering intelligence, and surveillance on an advanced level, effectively establishing an aerial force. Yet, despite possessing all this, he did not arrogantly claim to be an invincible power; rather, the context shows that when his military power increased, instead of boasting, he told his people: 'O people, we have been taught the language of birds and have been given of all things necessary for progress; indeed, this is a manifest favor of Allah.' The meaning is that the true right of this manifest favor is that we should become highly grateful and obedient to Him. Although the Torah presents Solomon as a worldly king and even attributes polytheism and idol-worship to him (refuted by the Qur'an), the Book of Kings still records that he always attributed his majesty and power to the grace of Allah and urged his people to remain grateful to Him.

The language of birds:
'We have been taught the language of birds' (Ullimna mantiqat-tayr) shows that birds possess speech and perception, and Solomon was blessed with a special knowledge of their communication. Our inability to understand their speech is no proof of its non-existence. The Qur'an explicitly states that everything in the universe glorifies Allah, though we do not understand their glorification. It also states that all beasts and birds are distinct communities like us. We have pointed to the implications of this in Surah an-Nahl. Even those who do not possess Solomon's knowledge know that animals utilize distinct sounds to express fear, love, anger, joy, grief, concern, and other feelings. Those who train animals for specific tasks understand their sounds and signs just as they understand human communication. Those who have scientifically studied animal behavior have uncovered amazing details showing that even a tiny ant is endowed by the Creator with such wisdom, foresight, and understanding as can teach humans many lessons. The tasks dogs perform today in tracking and surveillance are highly amazing. When humans can discover so many secrets of animals through study and experience, there is nothing surprising if Allah blessed Solomon with a special knowledge of the language of birds. It should be noted that speech is formed by the combination of sounds. Creatures possessing perception and feelings utilize various sounds to express them, which forms their language, alongside non-verbal signs and gestures.

In the phrase 'the hosts of Solomon were gathered,' a word is omitted in Arabic, meaning: 'The hosts of Solomon were gathered for his inspection and review.'

The hosts of Solomon:
'Waza'a' literally means to prevent, restrain, or control, and is used in this sense in verse 19. When used in relation to an army, it means to organize, classify, and arrange in ranks.

This describes an inspection of Solomon's forces, where his entire army consisting of jinn, humans, and birds was gathered before him. 'So they were set in rows' (fahum yooza'oon) means the purpose of this gathering was their organization and classification. The mention of this parade, as will be clear from the upcoming verses, is intended to show that Solomon's army consisted of highly capable humans, jinn, and birds. Yet, when his forces displayed their amazing capabilities, instead of indulging in pride and proclaiming himself invincible, Solomon humbly bowed before his Lord, praying: 'My Lord, hold me back lest I deviate, and enable me to remain always a grateful and obedient servant.'

Explanation of 'the Valley of the Ants':
'Wadi-an-Naml' (the Valley of the Ants) could refer to a specific valley known by this name, or the Qur'an may have referred to it as such due to the abundance of ants there. Ants and similar insects build their colonies in regions whose environment is suitable for them.

Solomon understood the communication of insects:
This occurred during the parade, when Solomon's forces were marching and reached the Valley of the Ants. An ant, sensing the danger, warned its fellows: 'O ants, enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his hosts crush you while they perceive not.' 'While they perceive not' (wa hum la yash'uroon) means: 'Before the marching of Solomon's grand army, even mountains are like dust; how could they perceive our tiny existence being crushed under their feet?'

The wonders of ants:
Leaving aside scientific discoveries, even an ordinary person observing a large colony of ants moving from one place to another is amazed by their military organization and discipline. It is clear that the entire host marches under a single leader. On both sides of the column, at short distances, their scouts and sentries remain active, performing surveillance duties. The moment they sense danger, they communicate it to the column, and the colony takes defensive measures. Their migration, when a colony permanently moves to a new location with all their food stores and young, is a sight to behold. I have observed their migrations closely on several occasions; describing these observations would make a long narrative.

While these behaviors are visible to all, scientific research has confirmed beyond doubt that ants, as the Qur'an states, are indeed communities like us. Some species of ants herd other insects and utilize them for their own needs. They cultivate fields, plant seeds, and store the harvest in underground chambers. They have organized armies that attack enemies under the command of specific officers, and they have structured systems for training and division of labor.

Although the statement attributed to the ant could have been communicated through signs—as the word 'qala' (said) is used in the Qur'an to denote communication through signs (as in Maryam: 37)—the apparent wording of the verse suggests an audible communication. It should be noted that the species of ants are not limited to those we are familiar with in our regions; scientific research shows that countless species exist in different lands, including both vocal and non-vocal ones. Just as Solomon understood the language of birds, he was blessed with the ability to hear and understand the communication of these insects through a God-given knowledge.

In the phrase 'So he smiled, laughing,' the word 'dahikan' denotes joy, happiness, and delight, as in Surah Hud, verse 71. A mere smile (tabassum) can sometimes carry a tone of disdain, but coupling it with 'laughing' (dahikan) clarifies that his smile was out of pure joy and delight.

The core reality for which this event is related:
'My Lord, enable me to be grateful...' (Awzi'nee an ashkura...). The definition of 'waza'a' has been explained above; here, it is used in the sense of holding back and controlling. Solomon prayed: 'My Lord, since You have blessed me with such abundance of knowledge that I can understand even the communication of insects, hold my reins in Your hand lest I fall into pride and deviate from Your path. Enable me to fulfill the rights of the great favor You have bestowed upon me and my parents, and let my actions be in accordance with Your pleasure, and join me with the righteous in the afterlife.'

This is the core reality for which this event is related. Those whose vision is limited to this worldly life and who are unmindful of God turn arrogant when they achieve success, as if it were their own achievement, and end up utilizing their talents and resources for satanic ends. In contrast, Solomon recognized every success and scientific advancement as a favor of his Lord, and remained always fearful of Him, striving to ensure his actions conformed to His pleasure.

Verses 20-21
The aerial force of Solomon: During the inspection of his avian forces, Solomon noticed that the hoopoe (hud-hud) was missing. Expressing his displeasure, he asked: 'What is the matter that I do not see the hoopoe? Is he present and I cannot see him, or is he indeed absent? He must bring me a clear excuse for his absence; otherwise, I will punish him severely or slaughter him.'

Based on contextual clues, some words are omitted before 'Or is he among the absent': 'Is he present and I cannot see him, or...'

'Sultanun-mubeen' literally means a clear proof or argument. Here, it is used in the sense of a valid, reasonable excuse.

This indicates that Solomon's army included trained birds of various species, which he utilized for carrying messages, gathering intelligence, and reconnaissance. Since he understood their communication, he utilized them far more effectively than modern scientists do.

Utilizing birds for intelligence gathering:
The practice of utilizing birds for carrying messages and gathering intelligence has existed in both ancient and modern times. Although the Jewish and Christian scriptures do not record this event in their accounts of Solomon, the story of Noah mentions that when he wanted to check if the flood waters had receded, he released a dove from the ark. After some time, the dove returned carrying a fresh olive branch in its beak, from which Noah concluded that the waters had receded.

This shows that the art of training birds for intelligence gathering and carrying messages is very ancient. While the carrier pigeon is well-known, the story of Noah shows that other birds are also highly capable. Modern science confirms this potential. The hoopoe is abundant in the region of Palestine and is highly intelligent, which is why Solomon utilized it in his forces.

The firm manner in which Solomon addressed the hoopoe's absence shows that these birds were subject to strict military discipline. They had to conform fully to the organizational rules, and any violation was subject to military regulations.

Verses 22-24
The hoopoe's report regarding the kingdom of Sheba:
'But he stayed not long' (Fa-makatha ghayra ba'eed). In my view, the subject of the verb is Solomon—meaning soon after Solomon expressed his displeasure, the hoopoe arrived and presented its report: 'I have grasped what you have not grasped, and I have come to you from Sheba with certain news.' Sheba (Saba) is the southwestern region of Arabia, where Yemen, Hadramaut, and Asir are located. The hoopoe's words did not mean that Solomon was completely ignorant of this kingdom; how could he be ignorant of a neighboring land when both the Torah and the Qur'an indicate that the Queen of Sheba was already somewhat aware of Solomon's fame and wisdom? Since Solomon's naval fleet traveled to distant lands, he must have had general knowledge of Sheba. The hoopoe meant: 'I have brought fresh, detailed intelligence regarding this kingdom that will add to your knowledge.' This is also reflected in 'I have grasped what you have not grasped,' meaning: 'I have gathered detailed information beyond what is generally known to you.' Since the hoopoe presented this as an excuse for its absence, it emphasized both the credibility and the novelty of its report. This is how ambassadors and intelligence officers present reports to their rulers, and it carries no disrespect toward Solomon's knowledge.

'Indeed, I found a woman...' (Innee wajadtu-mra'atan...). Regarding the ruler of the land, it reported that a woman was ruling over them, and the tone suggests that a female ruler was considered an unusual thing. Regarding the country's resources, it reported: 'and she has been given of all things' (wa ootiyat min kulli shay'), meaning all things necessary for prosperity and development. Regarding the Queen's majesty, it reported: 'and she has a great throne' (wa laha 'arshun 'azeem). Since the thrones of ancient kings were decorated with gold, silver, and jewels, their grandeur was measured by these materials.

Regarding their religious and moral state, it reported that the Queen and her people were sun-worshippers, and that Satan had made this sun-worship and their other actions so fair-seeming to them that they could not see the true path of monotheism and God-worship.

Verses 25-26
A parenthetical addition:
In my view, these two verses are not part of the hoopoe's report, but are a parenthetical addition by Allah, similar to other examples in the Qur'an. Following the hoopoe's statement 'and he averted them from the path,' Allah added these words to make the discourse complete. The meaning is: 'Satan has blocked them from worshipping the God who brings to light what is hidden in the heavens and the earth, and who knows what you conceal and what you declare. There is no deity but Him, the Lord of the Great Throne.'

There are clear textual clues showing that this is a parenthetical addition; the second-person plural verbs 'what you conceal and what you declare' would be completely out of place in the mouth of the hoopoe. They could only be uttered by Allah.

In 'Who brings to light what is hidden in the heavens and the earth,' 'khab'' refers to hidden things. Meaning, it is Allah who brings to light all hidden things, yet the foolish worship others. The hidden things of the heavens refer specifically to the sun, moon, stars, and rain, among which the sun was worshipped as the supreme deity by the people of Sheba. The hidden things of the earth refer to vegetation, rivers, and treasures. The text states that the Creator and Owner of all these is Allah alone, and He is fully aware of all your secrets, so what is the basis for worshipping others?

'Allah - there is no deity except Him, Lord of the Great Throne' (Allahu la ilaha illa Huwa Rabbul-'Arshil-'Azeem) means that the true Owner of the Great Throne, which encompasses the heavens and the earth, is He alone, so worship Him. Before His Throne, no other throne—regardless of its grandeur—holds any value.

Verses 27-28
A logical deduction:
Solomon said: 'We will see whether you have spoken the truth or are of the liars. Take this letter of mine and deliver it to them. Then draw back and see what response they return.' This means that they will surely show some reaction to the letter, which will help determine whether the hoopoe was boasting or speaking the truth. This indicates that the matter of the kingdom of Sheba was already under Solomon's consideration, and it is likely that the hoopoe flew to that region to gather intelligence because of this. Without a pre-existing plan, it is highly unlikely that Solomon would send a decisive ultimatum to the Queen of Sheba merely to test a hoopoe's truthfulness. The nature of the letter, as mentioned ahead, is indeed that of an ultimatum.

The instruction 'Then turn away from them and see' means: 'Deliver the letter and do not return immediately, but try to observe their reaction.' Some commentators have suggested that Solomon instructed the hoopoe to draw back so that they could discuss the letter freely without feeling any hesitation in the presence of an foreign messenger. However, this does not seem correct. While utilizing birds for carrying messages was known, there is no evidence that the people of Sheba knew at that stage that Solomon's messenger hoopoe could actually hear and understand their conversation.

Verses 29-30
Solomon's letter to the Queen of Sheba:
Upon receiving the letter, the Queen immediately consulted her courtiers, saying: 'O chiefs, indeed there has been delivered to me a noble letter' (Innee ulqiya ilayya kitabun kareem). This statement highlights two points:

First: The practice of sending letters through birds was a known method, at least among rulers. Had this not been the case, the Queen would have been astonished by a letter arriving without a human messenger, and her courtiers would have expressed surprise. Yet, neither the Queen nor her courtiers showed any surprise at the manner of its delivery; she mentioned it as a normal occurrence, and they listened to it as such.

Second: The Queen described the letter as 'noble' (kareem), which indicates that the writer—Solomon—already held a high status of respect and majesty in their hearts. Testimonies of this are present in the Torah, the Talmud, and the upcoming verses of the Qur'an.

'Indeed, it is from Solomon, and indeed, it is in the name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful' (Innahu min Sulaymana wa innahu Bismillahir-Rahmanir-Rahim). The Queen presented the summary of the letter: that it was from Solomon and began with 'Bismillahir-Rahmanir-Rahim'. It was the practice of ancient rulers to begin their letters and decrees with the names of the sender and recipient. Since the Queen presented a summary, she specifically mentioned the sender's name and the opening invocation. This throws light on the history of the Bismillah, showing that the Queen gave special importance to this opening and wanted her courtiers to understand the spiritual essence of the invitation.

'Be not haughty against me but come to me in submission' (Alla ta'loo 'alayya wa'toonee muslimeen). The Queen explained the core demand of Solomon's letter: 'Do not show arrogance against me, but submit to my authority.'

While contextual clues suggest the Queen presented a summary, it is also a fact that ancient decrees were highly concise and brief. This is also evident from the letters of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Therefore, even if Solomon's letter was exactly as short as quoted, there is nothing surprising about it.

Solomon's demand to the people of Sheba:
The Book of Kings in the Torah records that during Solomon's reign, the surrounding kingdoms either submitted to his authority of their own accord or were brought under his control through military strength. He acted on the principle that the dominance of disbelief and polytheism on earth is a grave source of corruption that must be subdued by the followers of truth. On this principle, he demanded that the people of Sheba submit to his authority and abandon their rebellion, so that he would not have to take military action against them. 'Muslimeen' here is used in the sense of obedience and submission, not necessarily embracing the faith of Islam. While a true Islamic government has no right to force non-Muslims to embrace the faith, it has the right to prevent false systems from holding political dominance, for the implementation of any law other than God's law on His earth is a form of rebellion and corruption.

The Queen of Sheba's consultation with her courtiers:
Having read the letter, the Queen sought the counsel of her courtiers: 'Give me your advice in this matter; I do not make a decision on any important issue until you are present and offer your counsel.' She likely expressed this to win their support, as she was not in favor of war against Solomon (as will be clear ahead) and wanted her nobles to think along the same lines.

It should be noted that no absolute monarchy is completely independent of the counsel of others; even Pharaoh consulted his nobles. Thus, the Queen's consultation does not mean her government was a democracy or a shura; it was an oligarchy of a specific kind.

The courtiers' advice:
The courtiers replied: 'We possess great strength and are fighters of great valor, so if you decide on war, we are ready to face the adversary, but the final decision is yours, so consider what you will command.'

Through 'possessors of strength,' they pointed to their numerical strength and weapons, and through 'fighters of great valor,' to their military skill and courage—the two essential elements for war. This shows that the courtiers favored war and presented their stance to the Queen.

قالت ان الملوک اذا دخلوا قریۃ افسدوھا و جعلوآ اعزۃ اھلھا اذلۃ وکذلک یفعلون (٣٤) 
The Queen's view:
The Queen did not agree with the courtiers' advice. She had heard of Solomon's majesty, wisdom, and military power, so she discouraged the idea of war, warning them of its consequences: 'When kings conquer a land, they completely destroy it and humiliate its noble citizens. If Solomon's forces conquer us, they will surely do the same.' The meaning is that avoiding war is the best course of action.

وانی مرسلۃ الیھم بھدیۃ فنظرۃ بم یرجمع المرسلون (٣٥)
The Queen advised a policy of peace and diplomacy over war, saying: 'I will first send gifts to Solomon through my envoys and see how he responds to our gesture of goodwill, and what answer our messengers bring back.'

'And see with what the messengers return' suggests that she had doubts about the success of this diplomatic gesture. She knew that Solomon was a man of principle and could not be bought with gifts, but she wanted to explore every avenue to avoid war and settle the issue peacefully, which shows her wisdom and foresight.

فلما جآء سلیمن قال المدونن بمال فمآ اتین اللہ خیر مما اتکم بل انتم بھدیتکم تفرحون (٣٦)
Solomon's response to the Queen's delegation:
In accordance with her plan, the Queen sent a delegation with valuable gifts to Solomon. The singular verb 'came' is used for the delegation. Meaning, when the delegation sent by her arrived before Solomon, he refused to accept their gifts, saying: 'Do you offer me wealth? What Allah has given me is far better than what He has given you. It is you who rejoice in your gifts.' The phrase 'what Allah has given me' includes the wealth, majesty, wisdom, and authority Solomon possessed. He explained that such gifts may please them, but they hold no value for him.

Solomon's message was: 'My demand is that you submit to my authority; I am not seeking mere diplomatic relations or trade pacts to be pleased with these gifts. I possess far more wealth than you, and my focus is on establishing the truth on earth. It is not permissible for me to accept gifts and tolerate the political dominance of disbelief on God's earth.'

ارجع الیھم فلنا تینھم بجنود لاقبل لھم بھا ولنخر جنھم منھا اذلۃ وھم صغرون (٣٧)
Solomon told the head of the delegation: 'Return to your Queen and her nobles. We will surely march against them with forces they cannot resist, and we will expel them from their land in humiliation, and they will be utterly subdued.'

'No power to resist' (la qibala lahum) means they lack the strength and resources to face his army. Solomon had learned from his messenger that the Queen's courtiers were proud of their military strength, so he warned them that their pride would be shattered before his forces.

Coupling 'humiliated' (adhillah) with 'utterly subdued' (wa hum saghiroon) suggests that his forces would crush their power so completely that they would never dare to think of war again. Sometimes a nation is defeated in war but retains its pride, but Solomon made it clear that his victory would leave them completely humbled.

قال یایھا الملوا ایکم یاتینی بعرشھا قبل ان یاتونی مسلمین (٣٨)
Solomon's plan:
After dismissing the envoys, Solomon concluded that they would not risk war and would soon submit to his demand. Before they arrived, he wanted to display a sign of the majesty of truth that would leave them completely awed. Therefore, he decided to bring the Queen's grand throne—which his messenger hoopoe had described—to his court. He asked his courtiers: 'O chiefs, which of you can bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?'

قال عفریت من الجن انا اتیک بہ قبل ان تقوم من مقامک دانی علیہ لقوی امین (٣٩)
'Ifreet' refers to one who is clever, resourceful, powerful, and a leader among his kind.

The offer of a jinn leader:
We have explained elsewhere that Solomon was blessed by Allah with the ability to subject jinn to his authority for various state services. When Solomon expressed his wish before his courtiers, a powerful leader among the jinn immediately offered his services, saying: 'I can bring it to you before you rise from your place, and indeed, I am strong enough to carry it and am trustworthy, so you can be sure I will not damage it or misplace any of its jewels.'

قال الذی عندہ علم من الکتب انا اتیک بہ قبل ان یرتد الیک طرفک فلما راہ مستقراً عندہ قال ھذا من فضل ربی لیبلونی اشکر امرا کفر ومن شکرفا فما یشکر نصہ ومن kefarfan rبی غنی کریماً (٤٠ )
The achievement of one who possessed knowledge of the Book:
When the jinn made his offer, the honor of a scholar of the Book was aroused. He said: 'The jinn promises to bring it before the assembly rises; I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you.' And in that instant, Solomon saw the throne placed right before him.

'Knowledge of the Book' refers to the knowledge of God's scripture and law—the Torah. It appears this scholar was proficient in the knowledge of the divine names and words, which we have explained in Surah al-Baqarah under the account of Harut and Marut. In our view, this refers to the knowledge of the divine names granted to the Children of Israel during their captivity in Babylon to counter sorcery. This knowledge is pure in itself, but it is a double-edged sword that can be misused for corrupt ends. In Surah al-Baqarah, it is recorded that the angels described this knowledge as a trial. During Solomon's reign, none dared to misuse it due to his strict accountability, but after him, the Jews became obsessed with it, neglecting the Torah and treating it as a form of magic, falsely claiming that Solomon's power was based on it.

This knowledge is not part of the Torah itself, but was granted under specific circumstances, as explained in Surah al-Baqarah. Here, 'one who possessed knowledge of the Book' (alladhee 'indahu 'ilmun minal-Kitab) highlights that those with true knowledge of the scripture possess such power through divine support as cannot be matched by the magic of jinn or humans.

Solomon's gratitude:
'And when he saw it placed before him...' (Falamma ra'ahu mustaqirran 'indahu...). This is the primary lesson of the event. When Solomon (peace be upon him) saw the Queen's throne placed right before him without any effort on his part or the help of any rebellious jinn, he did not turn arrogant. Instead, he immediately exclaimed: 'This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me whether I will be grateful or ungrateful. Whoever is grateful is grateful only for his own benefit, and whoever is ungrateful—my Lord is Self-Sufficient and Generous.' He recognized that a person's gratitude does not benefit God, but benefits the person himself, and that his Lord is Generous, granting blessings even to those who misuse them.

قال نکرہ لھا عرشھا نظرا تھتدی ام تکون من الذین لایھتدون (٤١ )
A display of Solomon's wisdom:
Bringing the throne was not an empty demonstration; Solomon wanted to show the Queen and her nobles the majesty of the truth Allah had granted him. He instructed his workers to alter the appearance of the throne slightly: 'Disguise her throne for her, so we may see whether she recognizes it or remains of those who are not guided.' He wanted to see if she would recognize her own throne despite the alterations, making it a test of her intelligence.

فلما جآءت قیل اھکذا عرشک قالت کانہ ہو ج واوتینا العلم من قبلھا وکنا ملمین (٤٢ )
The Queen of Sheba in Solomon's court:
Following the return of her delegation, the Queen decided to visit Solomon directly. The Torah and the Qur'an indicate that she was highly impressed by Solomon's wisdom, and wanted to benefit from his knowledge and find a peaceful solution to their political situation. She arrived with her ministers and nobles, and there she was presented with the test. Pointing to the altered throne, they asked: 'Is your throne like this?' The Queen was stunned, but answered cautiously: 'It is as if it were the same.' She then added openly: 'And we were given knowledge before this, and we have been in submission.' The pronoun in 'before this' refers to this miracle. Her words show that she and her nobles were already convinced of Solomon's wisdom and authority before this demonstration, and that they had already submitted to his authority in their hearts.

وصدھا ماکانت تعبد من دون اللہ ط انھا انت من قوم کفرین (٤٣ )
Exposing the barriers to her faith:
The Qur'an explains why the Queen had remained attached to a false religion despite being convinced of Solomon's wisdom and submitting to him in her heart: 'And that which she worshipped besides Allah had averted her; indeed, she was from a disbelieving people.' It means that the bonds of ancestral traditions and national identity are not easily broken. Many recognize the truth but are so bound by their social environment that they lack the courage to break free. Only those blessed with divine guidance succeed in removing these barriers. While this is difficult for everyone, it is doubly difficult for leaders, who often become slaves to their followers while claiming to lead them.

قیل لھا ادخلی الصرح فلما راتہ حسبتہ لجۃ وکشفت عن ساقیھا قال انہ صرح ممردمن قواریر قالت رب انی ظلمت نفسی و اسلمت مع سلیمن للہ رب العلمین (٤٤ )
The Queen of Sheba's astonishment in Solomon's glass palace:
Following this, the Queen was invited to enter the palace. As she entered, she was awed. Looking at the floor, she mistook it for a deep pool of water and instinctively pulled up her skirt to cross it. Solomon (peace be upon him) immediately reassured her, saying: 'This is not a pool of water; it is a palace paved with smooth glass.'

This highlights that Solomon's architectural achievements were so advanced that even a wealthy queen like the Queen of Sheba was astonished and made a natural mistake, proving she had never witnessed such a sight before.

The palace floor was constructed of thick glass tiles with running water beneath them, making it appear like a pool to anyone entering it for the first time. An ordinary person entering a grand city palace often experiences such astonishment; Solomon's palace was so magnificent that even the Queen of Sheba appeared like an unlettered villager before it.

The Queen of Sheba's declaration:
'She said: "My Lord, indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to Allah, Lord of the worlds"' (Qalat Rabbi innee zalamtu nafsee wa aslamtu ma'a Sulaymana lillahi Rabbil-Alameen). Witnessing Solomon's wisdom on one hand and his grand majesty on the other, the Queen exclaimed: 'My Lord, I have wronged myself by remaining attached to polytheism and ancestral traditions. Now, I submit with Solomon to the Lord of the worlds.' Through this declaration, she submitted herself to Allah and her kingdom to Solomon's authority. This shows that architectural majesty can be a sign of Allah when accompanied by humility, gratitude, and devotion, as displayed by Solomon. It guides people to the truth instead of blinding them. Without this spiritual element, such palaces become symbols of pride and tyranny, regardless of whether they are called the White House.

Humility and royal majesty:
Regarding Solomon's palace, it should be noted that it was constructed neither through foreign aid and loans nor by starving his people. It was built at a time when his kingdom was highly prosperous, so much so that gold was abundant and silver held little value. Furthermore, as will be clear from Surah Saba, Solomon constructed not only grand palaces but also massive public kitchens where the needy were provided with food. Therefore, architectural progress is not evil in itself; just as there is no harm in royal majesty when accompanied by humility, there is no harm in grand constructions when accompanied by public welfare. The evil lies in imbalance, and avoiding this imbalance requires great divine support.

The Queen of Sheba in the Torah:
This story is not recorded in such detail in the Torah, but its key elements are present. The Book of Kings describes her visit as follows:

'When the queen of Sheba heard about the fame of Solomon and his relationship to the name of the Lord, she came to test Solomon with hard questions... She arrived at Jerusalem with a very great caravan—with camels carrying spices, large quantities of gold, and precious stones—and when she came to Solomon, she talked with him about all that she had on her mind... When the queen of Sheba saw all the wisdom of Solomon and the palace he had built, the food on his table, the seating of his officials, the attending servants in their robes, his cupbearers, and the burnt offerings he made at the temple of the Lord, she was overwhelmed... She said to the king, "The report I heard in my own country about your achievements and your wisdom is true. But I did not believe these things until I came and saw with my own eyes. Indeed, not even half was told me; in wisdom and wealth you have far exceeded the report I heard. How happy your people must be! How happy your officials, who continually stand before you and hear your wisdom!"... King Solomon gave the queen of Sheba all she desired and asked for, besides what he had given her out of his royal bounty. Then she left and returned with her retinue to her own country.' (1 Kings 10:1-13)

Although the Torah's account is incomplete, the common thread is clear.

The Torah's account indicates that the Queen subsequently returned to her own country, which is highly consistent with the context, and nothing in the Qur'an contradicts this. Once she had submitted to Solomon's authority in both religion and politics, and since her nobles were of the same mind and she was a respected ruler among her people, it was best for her to return to her country to run her government on the principles taught by Solomon. Some traditional reports suggesting that Solomon married her and took her into his harem are not authentic and are weak in narration.

The upcoming subject... Verses 45-58
Above, the stories of Pharaoh's rebellion, Solomon's gratitude, and the Queen of Sheba's wisdom were related, showing the end of pride and the reward of humility. Now, two ancient nations of Arabia—Thamud and Lot's people—are mentioned, so that the Quraysh may not think these events concern only distant lands like Egypt, Palestine, and Yemen. Rather, they should learn from the end of the nations within their own land, whose ruined palaces still stand before them as a warning. Read the verses in this light.

ولقد ارسلنا الی ثمود اخاتھم صلحاً ان اعبدواللہ فاذا ھم خریقن یختصمون (45)
Salih's call and the response:
Salih called his people to worship and obey Allah alone. This was a clear call that left no room for dispute, and everyone should have welcomed it. But instead, some believed while the wicked chiefs rose in opposition, dividing the nation into two disputing groups.

قال یقوم لم تسغجلون بالستیۃ قبل الحسنۃ لولا تسغفرون اللہ لعلکم ترحمون (46)
The common tactic of the opponents, as explained throughout the Qur'an, has always been to demand the immediate descent of the promised punishment to prove the messenger's truthfulness. The nation of Salih made the same demand. Salih pleaded with them compassionately: 'O people, I am calling you to Allah's mercy, so why do you hasten toward His punishment? Why do you not seek His forgiveness instead, so that you may deserve His mercy?' 'Evil' (sayyi'ah) here refers to punishment, and 'good' (hasanah) refers to repentance and its reward of mercy. It should be noted that the Quraysh were displaying the exact same attitude at this stage, demanding that if their rejection of the Qur'an would lead to their destruction, they should be shown a sign of it first. They are told through Salih's words: 'When food is offered to you, do not ask for stones. Those who demanded stones instead of food met a disastrous end, and it will be your own ruin if you do not learn from them.'

قالوا ظیرنابک وبمن معک قال طئرکم عند اللہ بل انتم قوم نفتون (47)
We have explained the divine law under Surah al-A'raf, verse 94, that during a messenger's mission, special trials are sent to turn the hearts of the people toward the messenger's call, warning them that if they reject him, they will face a decisive punishment. Allah arranged this to make the atmosphere suitable for the call, but instead of learning from these trials, the people allowed their leaders to convince them that such trials are normal events of life, or they blamed them on the 'bad omen' of the messenger and his companions. They would say: 'These trials are due to their opposition to our gods and ancestral religion; the anger of our gods has struck them, and we are suffering because of them.' We have discussed this in detail in previous Surahs. This verse points to this situation: when they faced trials designed to turn their hearts toward the truth, they blamed their suffering on the bad omen of Salih and his companions, showing their absolute stubbornness.

'He said: "Your omen is with Allah. Rather, you are a people being tried"' (Qala ta'irukum 'indallahi bal antum qawmun tuftanoon). The meaning of 'ta'ir' (omen/fortune) has been explained under Surah al-A'raf, verse 131.

Salih replied: 'You attribute these trials to our bad omen, but their source is the plan, decree, and wisdom of Allah. They are not due to us, but are trials sent by Allah to see if you possess any sincerity to accept the truth, or if you are completely dead and fit only for destruction!'

وکان فی المدینۃ تسعۃ رھط یفسدون فی الارض ولایصلحون (48)
Meaning of 'raht':
'Raht' means a clan or a family. This word appeared in the story of Shu'ayb in Surah Hud: 'And indeed, we see you among us as weak, and if not for your family (raht), we would have stoned you' (Hud: 91). It is used for both singular and plural, and those who translate it as 'individuals' are incorrect.

The power of the mufsideen:
This highlights the strength of the opponents: there were nine prominent families in the city who were all actively opposing Salih's call, while Salih stood alone among them. 'They caused corruption on earth and would not reform' (yufsidoona fil-ardi wa la yuslihoon) means these families were constantly engaged in corruption and refused to accept any reform. The only way to reform is to submit to Allah's authority; therefore, opposing the messenger's call is the ultimate form of corruption.

قالوا تفاسموا باللہ لبیتنہ واھلہ ثم لنقولن بولیہ ما شھدنا مھلک اھلہ وانا الصدقون (49)
The conspiracy of the chiefs against Salih:
'Swear by Allah' (Tafasamoo billahi) here is used as an oath. These corrupt families swore to each other by Allah: 'We will attack Salih and his family by night and kill them, and then we will tell his heirs that we did not witness the destruction of his family, and we will assure them of our innocence.' They planned this secret attack because they did not have the courage to kill Salih openly, fearing a tribal war. In tribal society, killing a member of another family was a grave matter that would spark a long war of قصاص (retribution). To avoid this, they planned a secret night attack, swearing to keep it secret so that Salih's heirs would be unable to identify the killers, or would be too intimidated by their collective strength to seek retribution. Recall that the chiefs of the Quraysh made a similar plan in Dar-un-Nadwah to assassinate the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) collectively, so that his clan would be unable to demand retribution. This story is related to reassure the Prophet that his opponents are making similar plans, but just as Allah protected Salih and his companions, He will protect him and his followers.

ومکروا مکرواو مکرنا مکر وھم لایشعرون فانظر کیف کان عاقبہ مکرھم انا رمرنھم وقومھم اجمعین (51-50)
Allah's plan against the conspirators:
The conspiracy of the chiefs was mentioned above. Allah's plan against them has been detailed in Surah ash-Shu'ara and previous Surahs: 'See how the end of their conspiracy was: We destroyed them and their entire nation.'

قتلک بیوتھم خاویۃ بما ظلموا ان فی ذلک لایۃ تقوم یعلمون وانجینا الذین امنوا وکالوا یتقون (53-52)
'These' (Tilka) indicates that the Quraysh are being referred to something visible to them. Meaning, this is not a far-off story; the ruins of Thamud's magnificent buildings are right there in your land. Since Thamud inherited the architectural skills of 'Ad, their ruined dwellings lay along the trade routes of the Quraysh, who passed by them frequently. 'Because of their injustice' (bi-ma zalamoo) means they brought this end upon themselves by rejecting Salih.

'Indeed, in that is a sign for a people who know' (Inna fee dhalika la-aayatan li-qawmin ya'lamoon) refers to those who wish to understand and learn a lesson. For those who choose to remain blind, no advice can be of benefit.

'And We saved those who believed and used to fear' (Wa anjaynalladheena aamanoo wa kanoo yattaqoon) refers to those who were saved, and their primary quality was their fear of Allah and His justice.

ولوطا ذقال یقومہ اقالون الفاحشۃ وانتم تبصرون (54)
Lot and his people:
This is connected to the previous story; thus, the verb 'We sent' is omitted: 'And We sent Lot...' 'Al-fahishah' refers to an open, shameless obscenity whose corrupt nature is undeniable.

'While you see' (wa antum tubsiroon) means: if a sin is committed in secret, it is understandable to some extent, but committing an open obscenity while you possess sight is the ultimate height of spiritual blindness. If a blind person steps into filth, he has an excuse, but what is your excuse when you are consuming filth while possessing sight!

اننکم لتاتون الرجال شھوۃ من دون الغسآء ط بل انتم قوم تجھلون (٥٥) 
The question here expresses surprise, disgust, and condemnation. 'Other than women' (min doonin-nisa') highlights their unnatural behavior: deviating from the natural, permissible way of satisfying desire is proof of a distorted nature. 'Jahl' (ignorance), as explained elsewhere, refers to being so overwhelmed by desire that one loses all reason and common sense: 'Indeed, you are a people who are ignorant' (Bal antum qawmun tajhaloon).

فماکان جواب قومہ الا ان قالوآ اخرجوآ ال لوط من تریتکو انھم اناس یتطھرون (56)
Instigating the public against Lot:
They had no logical argument to defend their obscenity, yet they refused to abandon it, so their only option was to silence Lot. The chiefs instigated the public: 'Expel the family of Lot from your town.' The sarcasm in 'Indeed, they are people who keep themselves pure' (Innahum unasun yatatahharoon) is obvious. To turn the public against Lot and his followers, they said: 'Look at them! According to them, our entire nation is corrupt, and only these few individuals are pure!' When an evil becomes socially accepted, it is easy to mock those who stand against it. Anyone who doubts this can try speaking against modern immodesty in a social gathering today.

We have explained elsewhere that 'Aal' includes family, household, and followers.

فانجینہ واھلہ الا امراتہ قدرنھا من الغبرین (57)
'Ahl' includes family, household, and close companions, and is used in this sense here.

Responsibility is in proportion to the blessings received:
'We destined her to be of those who remained behind' (qaddarnaha minal-ghabireen) indicates that Lot's wife was deprived of salvation. Since she chose to side with the wrongdoers despite having the best opportunity for guidance, she deserved His wrath more than others. The more opportunities for guidance Allah grants to a person, the more severe the punishment if they reject them. In Allah's law, responsibility is in proportion to the blessings received.

The end of Lot's people:
Meaning, Allah saved Lot and his followers, and upon the rest of the nation: 'And We rained upon them a rain' (Wa amtarna 'alayhim mataran), referring to the rain of stones detailed in previous Surahs.

'And quite evil was the rain of those who were warned' highlights that this destructive rain was the result of their rejecting the messenger after complete proof had been established. This points to the fact that, like Thamud, the towns of Lot's people lay along the trade routes of the Quraysh, who are urged to learn from these ruins.

The upcoming subject... Verses 59-93
Conclusion of the Surah
Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, summarizing the lessons of the previous accounts. The Qur'an's call of monotheism is presented with powerful arguments from the universe and human nature. The actual cause of the deniers' rejection is exposed: their doubt regarding the afterlife, which is an undeniable reality supported by all the signs of the universe. This connects the conclusion of the Surah with its introduction.

Throughout the discourse, and particularly at the end, the Prophet is consoled: do not worry about their objections and demands. Your duty is not to guide the blind or make the deaf hear; tell them clearly that your duty is only to convey this Qur'an. If they refuse to believe, they will soon witness the consequences predicted by the Qur'an. Read the verses in this light.

قل الحمد للہ وسلم علی عبادہ الذین اصطفی، اللہ خیرا ماً یشرکون (59)
An elegant transition:
This verse serves as an elegant transition between the previous accounts and the upcoming discourse. The stories related above highlight two key realities: first, Allah has not abandoned His creation, but His justice and mercy are constantly manifested in it, punishing the rebellious and saving His chosen servants.

Second: All authority and power belong to Allah alone, and the false deities have no reality. This summary serves to introduce the upcoming discourse on monotheism: 'Say: "Praise be to Allah, and peace be upon His servants whom He has chosen. Is Allah better or what they associate with Him?"' It means that praise belongs to Allah who displayed His justice and mercy, and safety is for His chosen servants whom He protects against all conspiracies. 'Is Allah better...' is the transition to the arguments for monotheism.

To understand the true force of this and the upcoming verses, keep in mind the specific style of Arabic where rhetorical questions are not meant to seek answers, but to silence, establish proof, and rebuke the opponent. The speaker presents the facts with great eloquence and moves forward without waiting for a response, as if there is no room to dispute them. In this style, some parts of the sentence are omitted, which the listener is expected to understand. While this style is highly eloquent in Arabic, we have filled in the omitted elements in the translation to make the meaning clear.

Provision of sustenance:
Before 'Or, Who created the heavens and the earth,' some words are omitted: 'Are your false deities better, who have no share in creation or sustenance, or the One who created the heavens and the earth and sent down rain from the sky, causing beautiful gardens to grow, whose trees you could never cause to grow?'

'Then We caused to grow thereby gardens of passing beauty' (fa-anbatna bihi hada'iqa dhata bahjah). The style transitions to the first-person plural ('We') to emphasize His grace, mercy, and rububiyyah.

'Is there a deity with Allah?' (A-ilahun ma'allah) means: 'Was there any partner with Him in performing these acts?' Obviously not, so on what basis do you worship others? Recall that the polytheists acknowledged Allah as the sole Creator and Provider.

Meaning of 'ya'diloon':
'Rather, they are a people who equate' (Bal hum qawmun ya'diloon). 'Ya'diloon' here is from 'adala', meaning to deviate or act unjustly. It means they have no argument to defend their false deities, but are simply unjust and deviate from the truth. The transition to the third-person plural ('they') expresses contempt and disapproval, as if turning away from them.

امن جعل الارض قراراوجعل خدلھا انھراً وجعل لھارواسی وجعل بین البحرین حاجزاً الہ مع اللہ ط بل اکثرھم لایعلمون
Here too, the opening part of the question is omitted: 'Are your false deities worthy of worship, or the One who made the earth a stable place?' The earth is described as a cradle or a bed in other places, and here the word '稳定' (qarar) is used.

'And made rivers run through it' means He provided rivers and springs on earth for your sustenance.

'And placed firm mountains upon it' to keep the earth stable on its axis so that it does not shake with you.

'And placed a barrier between the two seas' (Wa ja'ala baynal-bahrayni hajiza). As explained elsewhere, this refers to sweet water rivers and salty seas meeting, yet Allah maintains an invisible barrier between them, preventing one from encroaching upon the other. The text asks: 'Was there any partner with Him in these acts of power and wisdom? If not, on what basis have they been made partners in His divinity?'

'Rather, most of them do not know' (bal aktharuhum la ya'lamoon). The third-person plural expresses sorrow that these obvious realities are ignored by the majority.

امن یجیب المضطراذا دعاہ ویکشف السوآء وریجعلکم خلفآء الارض ط والہ مع اللہ ط قلیلاً ماتذکرون (62)
Some realities regarding the acceptance of prayer:
Here too, the opening part of the question is omitted: 'Are your false deities better, who can neither hear, see, nor help, or the One who responds to the distressed and removes suffering?' Meaning, whoever's prayer is accepted, it is accepted by Him alone. It should be noted that this is coupled with the condition of divine will in other places, meaning He removes suffering in accordance with His wisdom. If a distressed person's prayer is not answered immediately, it is deferred for a more appropriate time, or answered in a better way, or recorded as a reward in the hereafter. This verse should be understood in the light of these principles.

The rise and fall of nations is by His command:
'And makes you successors of the earth' (wa yaj'alukum khulafa'al-ard) refers to one nation succeeding another on earth. This points to the fact that the rise and fall of nations are not accidental events, but occur by His command and wisdom. This warnings the Quraysh: 'Your current political dominance is a favor from Allah to test your conduct. If you adopt the same rebellious attitude as previous nations, you will face the same end, for His law is the same for all.'

The unmindfulness of nations:
'Is there a deity with Allah? Little do you remember' (A-ilahun ma'allah qaleelan ma tadhakkaroon) means they cannot point to any partner in these acts, yet they are unmindful. When they face trials, they call upon Him, but once the trials are removed, they return to their old ways, forgetting their promises. Similarly, they ignore the lessons of history until the punishment strikes them. Thus, there is a close connection between 'He responds to the distressed' and 'makes you successors of the earth', showing that they learn from neither individual trials nor historical lessons.

God's authority is over both land and sea:
Here too, the opening part of the question is omitted: 'Are your false deities worthy of worship, or the One who guides you in the darkness of land and sea and sends the winds as glad tidings before His mercy?' Allah's arrangement for guidance in the dark is explained as: 'And by the stars they are guided' (an-Nahl). He placed signs on earth and stars in the sky to guide travelers, proving His mercy and sole authority over both land and sea. Similarly, He sends the winds to gather clouds and bring rain to dead lands. How can anyone suggest there are separate gods for land, sea, rain, and wind? This cosmic harmony proves that there is only one Creator, and He is free from the partners they associate with Him.

The logical conclusion of an obvious reality:
Here too, the opening part of the question is omitted: 'Are your false deities worthy of worship, or the One who originates creation?' Coupling this with 'and then repeats it' (thumma yu'eeduha) highlights the logical necessity: since He originated creation, He can repeat it, and He must do so to ensure creation is not a purposeless exercise. This is presented as an obvious conclusion: since you cannot deny the origination of creation, how can you doubt its repetition?

'And Who provides for you from the heaven and the earth' (Wa man yarzuqukum minas-sama'i wal-ard) means He uses both the sky (rain) and the earth (vegetation) for your sustenance. Can anyone claim a partner in these acts?

Conclusion:
'Say: "Produce your proof, if you should be truthful"' (Qul hatoo burhanakum in kuntum sadiqeen). These acts require an All-Wise, All-Powerful Creator, which is an undeniable reality. If you claim there are other gods alongside Him, the responsibility lies on you to produce proof. If you have any argument, produce it.

The polytheists must produce proof:
This summary shows that those who reject God must answer these questions, and those who associate partners with Him must produce proof. The responsibility of proof lies entirely on the deniers and the polytheists, not on the believers who acknowledge an obvious reality.

This is the final word in the discourse on monotheism: 'Say: "None in the heavens and the earth knows the unseen except Allah."' Since even the celestial beings do not know the unseen, how can humans know it? The text adds: 'and they do not perceive when they will be resurrected.' When they do not even know when they will be resurrected, what is the basis for worshipping them in hope of their intercession? This has been explained in Surah an-Nahl, verses 20-21.

'Iddaraka' is originally 'tadaraka', where 'ta' was assimilated into 'da'. It means they gathered together. In Arabic, it is also used in the sense of being confused or mixed up, as gathering diverse things together leads to confusion.

The intellectual confusion of the Arab polytheists regarding the afterlife:
We have explained that the stance of the Arab polytheists regarding the afterlife was not one of outright rejection, but of deep intellectual confusion. They did not want to accept it, yet they could find no logical basis to deny it completely. While some extreme individuals denied it out of stubbornness, the majority suffered from this confusion, treating it as highly improbable: 'How can we be resurrected after we have died and turned to dust?' Yet, to reassure themselves, they would say: 'Even if we are resurrected, our gods will intercede to save us, so we need not worry.' The Qur'an describes this state as 'their knowledge was arrested regarding the Hereafter' (iddaraka 'ilmuhum fil-akhirah), which is a highly accurate description.

This verse is placed here to reflect the introduction (verse 3): although this message is clear, those who do not wish to believe in the afterlife will not accept it. After presenting undeniable arguments for monotheism, the text states that their rejection is not because these realities are unclear, but because they are in doubt and have turned blind to the afterlife.

Rejecting the truth stems from a desire to avoid the moral responsibility that comes with accepting the afterlife. To avoid this responsibility, they seek doubts and excuses, which leads to spiritual blindness. The Qur'an details this progressive decline: they began with a desire to deny the afterlife, which led to doubt, and ultimately to absolute spiritual blindness.

68-67
Second, they say: 'We have been hearing about this resurrection for a long time; it was promised to our forefathers as well, but it never came.' They conclude that these are nothing but legends of the past repeated in every age.

قل سیزوا فی الارض فانظروا کیف کان عاقبۃ المجرمین (69)
Opening the eyes to history:
This is the cure for their spiritual blindness: 'Say: "Travel through the land and see what was the end of the criminals."' Look at the ruins of past nations and see how the promised punishment overtook them. These ruins are preserved on earth as a warning and a lesson, showing that the warnings of the messengers are a reality, not legends. It also shows that since the criminals were punished in this world, Allah will surely establish a Day of complete justice and mercy.

ولاتحزن علیھم ولاتکن فی ضیق مما یمکرون (70)
Consolation for the Prophet:
This consoles the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'Do not grieve over them.' You are offering them life, and they demand destruction; this is their own loss. Do not exhaust yourself over their stubbornness.

Similarly, do not be distressed by the conspiracies they make against your companions and your call. All their plans are in God's grasp; He will foil them and protect you from every evil.

Verses 71-73
'Radifa' is used both with and without the preposition 'li' in Arabic, meaning to follow closely behind.

The deniers' demand for punishment:
The messengers warned their nations of two punishments: the worldly punishment that strikes upon their rejection, and the punishment of the hereafter. The nations responded: 'When is this promise to be fulfilled, if you are truthful?' They demanded to see the promised punishment as proof of his truthfulness.

'Say: "Perhaps it is close behind you..."' (Qul 'asa an...). 'That which you hasten' refers to the Day of Judgment, as mentioned in verse 68. 'A part of it' refers to the worldly punishment that follows rejection. Solomon warned them: 'Do not hasten the punishment; its occurrence now depends on your conduct. If you do not believe, a model of the ultimate justice will strike you.' The word 'perhaps' ('asa) is used because the descent of punishment depended on their conduct; indeed, since the majority of the Quraysh ultimately embraced Islam, they were spared the total destruction that befell previous nations.

'And indeed, your Lord is full of bounty to the people, but most of them are not grateful' (Wa inna Rabbaka la-dhoo fadlin 'alan-nasi wa lakinna aktharahum la yashkuroon). This is an expression of sorrow over those who demand punishment: the respite Allah grants them is out of His grace so that they may reform, but they treat this delay as proof of the Prophet's falsehood, thus inviting His wrath.

Verses 74-75
Consolation for the Prophet and a warning to the opponents:
This consoles the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and warnings his opponents: 'Your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they declare.' Meaning, He will foil their conspiracies and protect you, reinforcing 'Do not be in distress because of what they conspire.'

'And there is nothing hidden...' is a warning to the opponents that every secret of the heavens and the earth is recorded in a clear Book; He will call them to account, and nothing can be hidden from Him.

Verses 76-78
This refers to the Jews. Although this Surah is primarily addressed to the Quraysh, the Jews—who initially watched the call from a distance—gradually began to support the Quraysh behind the scenes. Therefore, the Qur'an addressed them as well, beginning with subtle references that became more explicit as their opposition grew. This verse contains a brief reference to them, which will become more detailed in subsequent Surahs.

It states that just as the Qur'an establishes proof against the Quraysh, it clarifies to the Children of Israel many of the issues in which they differed, and 'it is a guidance and mercy for the believers.' If they believe, it will lead them out of their confusion to the straight path.
