'Indeed, your Lord will judge between them by His judgment' warnings them that while Allah currently offers them guidance through this Book, a Day will come when He will judge between them with His final, decisive decree. Context shows this refers to the Day of Judgment.

'And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Knowing' points to His attributes: they should know that He is Mighty, so none can escape Him, and Knowing, so nothing is hidden from Him.

This same theme appeared in Surah Yunus: 'And We settled the Children of Israel in an honorable settlement and provided them with good things, and they did not differ until knowledge came to them. Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ' (Yunus: 93).

In Surah Hud: 'And We had given Moses the Scripture, but difference arose therein. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them. And indeed they are, concerning it, in disquieting doubt' (Hud: 110).

The difference referred to here has been explained under those verses and in Surah al-Baqarah. The Torah was not only distorted but also became a collection of contradictory narratives, making it impossible to distinguish truth from falsehood. Thus, the Jews lost the guidance and fell into darkness. The Qur'an sought to lead them out of this darkness, but they chose to oppose it.

Verses 79-81
Consolation for the Prophet:
This consoles the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'So trust in Allah; indeed, you are upon the clear truth.' The truth will ultimately triumph, and your opponents—whether the Quraysh or the Jews—cannot match you with their falsehood. Allah will foil their plans, and His word will prevail.

'Indeed, you cannot make the dead hear...' means you should not exhaust yourself over their refusal to listen. Your duty is not to make the dead or the deaf hear, especially when they turn their backs and flee. The condition 'when they turn their backs, fleeing' suggests that even the deaf might understand some signs if they pay attention, but these people are both deaf and fleeing, so how can they be guided?

'And you cannot guide the blind away from their error' (Wa ma anta bi-hadiyal-'umyi 'an dalalatihim). The preposition 'an' shows that the verb 'guide' carries the sense of turning them away from their error to guidance.

'You will only make hear those who believe in Our verses, for they are in submission' (In tusmi'u illa man yu'minu bi-aayatina fahum muslimoon). 'Believe' here carries the sense of desiring to believe. It means that those who have no desire to listen will never value this blessing, while those who seek the truth will believe and submit to their Lord.

The divine law regarding guidance:
Remember that Allah has endowed every human being with an inherent desire for guidance. The divine law is that those who value this desire are blessed with further guidance, while those who ignore it are deprived of it, and their inherent desire dies out. Lord Jesus expressed this as: 'From the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away.'

Verse 82
The sign of judgment:
'And when the word befalls them' (Wa idha waqa'al-qawlu 'alayhim) means when the divine decree is issued regarding them and the time for their punishment arrives.

The pronoun in 'them' refers to the Quraysh, who were demanding a sign of the promised punishment, as mentioned in verses 71-73.

In Surah ar-Room, verse 35: 'Or have We sent down to them an authority (sultan) that speaks of what they associate with Him?' Here, 'speaks' (yatakallamu) does not mean verbal speech, but serving as a clear proof or sign.

It states that if these rebels refuse to believe until they witness a sign, showing them a sign is not difficult for God. He is currently granting them respite to reform, offering them guidance through His Messenger and Book. But once the time for respite passes and the decree of punishment is issued, Allah does not need any elaborate arrangement; He will bring forth a beast from the earth that will serve as a sign of their impending doom, testifying that they refused to believe in His signs. This indicates that while Allah currently offers guidance, He can easily bring forth a sign from the earth to seal their fate.

An example of such a sign in history is the she-camel of Thamud. When they refused to believe and demanded a sign, Salih designated a she-camel as the sign of punishment; the moment they harmed her, the punishment descended upon them.

This explanation is clear from the context and style of the Qur'an. Traditional commentaries discuss a specific 'Beast of the Earth' (Dabbat-ul-Ard) appearing near the Resurrection, but in our view, those reports concern the signs of the Hour and should be evaluated against the principles of Hadith criticism.

It should be noted that this threat was conditional upon the decree of punishment being issued. Had the Quraysh persisted in their disbelief, they would have faced this total destruction, but since the majority of them embraced Islam, they were spared, and only their wicked chiefs were eliminated in battle.

The gathering of the deniers on the Day of Judgment:
This is a reminder of the Day of Judgment, when Allah will gather from every nation a host of those who rejected His signs, and they will be organized. The word 'yooza'oon' appeared in verse 17, meaning they will be classified into ranks. This classification will be based on the nature and gravity of their crimes, and each rank will be directed to its designated gate of Hell: 'It has seven gates, for each gate is a partitioned portion' (al-Hijr: 44).

'A host' (fawjan) highlights their large numbers, indicating that despite their vast numbers, gathering them is simple for God, and there will be no lack of space in Hell, which will digest them all and still ask: 'Are there any more?'

A question to the deniers:
When the criminals are gathered, Allah will ask them: 'Did you reject My verses when you had not encompassed them in knowledge, or what was it that you were doing?' Meaning, had you rejected the Resurrection on the basis of a complete, all-encompassing knowledge of the universe, you might have had an excuse. But since you lacked such knowledge, what is your excuse for rejecting My warnings?

This highlights that to accept a truth, it is sufficient that its signs are present in intellect, nature, and the universe. We cannot reject a truth merely because our knowledge does not encompass all its aspects. Rejecting it on that basis is akin to claiming all-encompassing knowledge, which belongs to the Creator alone. Such a person is like one who denies the existence of the Himalayas because he has not seen every peak and valley.

بما ظلموا فھولا ینطقون (85)
The helplessness of the deniers:
Meaning, they will find no excuse to offer in response to His question. Their mouths will be sealed, and the decree of punishment will be implemented against them. Here too, 'befalls them' (waqa'al-qawlu) is used, referring to the final decree casting them into Hell.

In Surah Ya-Seen, verse 65: 'This Day We will seal over their mouths, and their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will testify...' This further explains their helplessness.

In this verse, according to standard Arabic usage, some words are omitted. In the first part, 'dark' (muthliman) is omitted, and in the second, 'you work' (ta'maloon) or a similar verb. The eloquence of this conciseness has been explained elsewhere.

'Believe' (yu'minoon) here carries the sense of desiring to believe.

Rebuking those who demand punishment:
This rebukes those who demand a sign of punishment: why do they persist in demanding destruction, instead of reflecting on the signs of the universe? Do they not see His power and wisdom in making the night dark for them to rest, and the day bright for them to work? 'Indeed, in that are signs for a people who believe' (Inna fee dhalika la-aayatin li-qawmin yu'minoon) means there are many signs for those who seek the truth.

The alternation of night and day, their distinct nature, and their utility are powerful signs of God's power, wisdom, rububiyyah, monotheism, and the Resurrection. The text focuses on the Resurrection: you witness a model of it every day. Just as you sleep every night and wake up every morning, you will be resurrected after death. God provides this daily model as a constant reminder, but those who choose to remain blind have no cure. Surah al-Furqan, verse 47, presents this: 'And He is the One who made the night for you as a cover, and sleep for rest, and made the day a resurrection (nushoor).' This is also reflected in the supplication taught by the Prophet upon waking up.

This is a reminder of the terror of that Day: when the Horn is blown, everyone will be terrified, 'except whom Allah wills' (illa man sha'allah)—referring to those who will be protected from this terror, as explained in verse 89: 'Whoever comes with a good deed will have better than it, and they, from the terror of that Day, will be safe.' 'And all will come to Him humbled' (Wa kullun atawhu dakhireen) means everyone, leaders and followers alike, will present themselves in complete humility. Only those protected by Allah will be saved from this humiliation.

This further details the terror of that Day: even the massive mountains, which appear firm, will pass away like clouds. The ancient Arab poets used to treat mountains as symbols of eternity, but the text states: 'This is the handiwork of Allah, who perfected all things' (Sun'allahil-ladhee atqana kulla shay'). Since He perfected them, He can make them pass away like clouds.

'Indeed, He is Aware of what you do' (Innahu khabeerun bi-ma taf'aloon) defines the purpose of relating this terror: Allah is fully aware of all your deeds, and He will call you to account.

Verses 89-90
This explains the logical end of 'Indeed, He is Aware of what you do': those who bring good deeds will have a great reward and will be safe from the terror of that Day, while those who bring evil deeds will be cast headlong into the Fire, receiving exactly what their deeds deserved, without any injustice.

Verses 91-92
Defining the Prophet's mission:
At the end, the Prophet is commanded to define his mission and call in a decisive manner, so that the Quraysh may choose their path. The verb 'Say' (Qul) is omitted before 'I have only been commanded...' as shown by 'So say: "I am only..."' and 'And say: "Praise be to Allah..."' ahead.

He is commanded to tell them: 'I have only been commanded to worship the Lord of this city, who made it sacred and to whom belongs all things, and I have been commanded to be of the Muslims.' This is a reference to the history of Makkah and the Kaaba: 'I have been sent to revive the monotheism for which this city was made sacred, so your opposition cannot turn me away from my duty. This city is the center of Abraham's creed, and my call to monotheism is the actual duty here.' 'And to Him belongs all things' means the worship of other deities in this sacred city is unlawful, and I disassociate myself from them. 'And I have been commanded to be of the Muslims' means I submit to Him alone.

'And to recite the Qur'an' means: 'I have no concern with the demands and objections you raise to distract me. My duty is only to recite this Qur'an to you; whoever is guided is guided for his own benefit, and whoever is deviated—I am only a warner, and my responsibility ends once I have warned you.' This reflects Abraham's prayer for a messenger who would recite His verses, purify them, and teach them the Book and wisdom. The Prophet is carrying out this very duty, and those who seek their own well-being should join him.

Meaning: 'Say: "Praise be to Allah, who has blessed me with this Qur'an, which I recite to you. If you reject it and demand signs, the time is near when He will show you His signs, and you will recognize them."' At that time, you will realize the truth of my warnings, but recognizing it then will be of no benefit.

'And your Lord is not unaware of what you do' warnings them not to mistake His respite for ignorance; He is aware of everything and will call them to account at the appointed time.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed by the grace of Allah. Praise be to Allah for this.
